summary: Joel Spolsky argues that all successful software products take approximately ten years to mature from initial development to widespread adoption. He illustrates this with Lotus Notes, which took 11 years from first code to hockey-stick growth, and cites Oracle, Windows NT, and Microsoft Word as similar examples. The first few years produce rapid, meaningful feature additions, but after about a decade the software becomes essentially feature-complete and further updates yield diminishing returns. Spolsky identifies six common business mistakes that stem from failing to understand this ten-year timeline, including the Get Big Fast bubble mentality, overhyping 1.0 releases, believing in 'Internet Time,' struggling with upgrade revenue after feature completion, shipping nothing for years, and releasing upgrades too frequently. His prescription is to plan for a decade of sustained development, ship early but quietly, and accept that version 1.0 will always be inadequate.
key_points: Serious software products consistently take about 10 years from first code to mass-market maturity, as demonstrated by Lotus Notes, Oracle, Windows NT, and Microsoft Office

After the first decade of meaningful feature additions, software becomes essentially feature-complete, and subsequent upgrades add little real value — creating a revenue problem for companies dependent on upgrade sales
Overhyping a 1.0 release permanently poisons public perception, because early adopters who see a mediocre first version rarely come back to check later improvements
The myth of 'Internet Time' confused faster release cycles with faster development — software wasn't being written faster, just shipped more frequently during the early feature-rich phase
The ideal strategy is to ship early and quietly, keep costs low with small teams, gather customer feedback over years, and plan for a decade of iterative improvement
money_quotes: Good software, like wine, takes time.
It takes a long time to write a good program, but when it's done, it's done.
With all due respect to my friends on the Office team, I can't help but feel that there hasn't been a useful new feature in Office since about 1995.
Software was not getting created any faster, it was just getting released more often.

If you can only afford 1 programmer at the beginning, the architecture is likely to be reasonably consistent and intelligent, instead of a big mishmash with dozens of conflicting ideas from hundreds of programmers that needs to be rewritten from scratch.
Make a ten year plan. Make sure you can survive for 10 years, because the software products that bring in a billion dollars a year all took that long.
You have to release early, incomplete versions — but don't overhype them or advertise them on the Super Bowl, because they're just not that good, no matter how smart you are.
Failure to understand the ten-year rule leads to crucial business mistakes.
key_insight: Successful software products universally require about ten years of iterative development to reach maturity, and businesses that ignore this timeline — whether by overhyping, overspending, or expecting instant results — are setting themselves up for failure.
